mechanism: This is a balancing loop (perceived temperature → gap from desired → valve adjustment → actual temperature) with a long (~20s) transport delay between the valve and the showerhead. The person acts on the temperature they feel NOW, which reflects the valve setting from 20s ago. Feeling cold, they crank hot; but the already-in-transit water keeps arriving, so by the time the hot water reaches them it overshoots warm — so they crank cold, and the cycle repeats. The delay forces the corrective action to chronically lag the system state, converting a stabilizing loop into an oscillator: temperature swings above and below the target, settling only slowly as they learn to wait out the pipe. It ends higher than the too-cold start, hunting toward (and around) the comfortable target. Reacting MORE aggressively to the gap raises the loop gain on a delayed feedback: each over-correction is larger relative to the lag, so amplitude grows and settling slows — it can diverge into sustained or worsening oscillation rather than settling faster. The leverage is reducing the gain (gentler adjustments / wait for the pipe), not increasing it.
